text: Every indie hacker has been here: “Cool idea, let me just set up auth, DB, CRUD, RBAC, docker, etc…”3 days later: no real logic, just boilerplate.Templates kill this overhead. Here’s a peek into my Python API template and why it saves me weeks.

raw.full_text: Every indie hacker has been here: “Cool idea, let me just set up auth, DB, CRUD, RBAC, docker, etc…”3 days later: no real logic, just boilerplate.Templates kill this overhead. Here’s a peek into my Python API template and why it saves me weeks.

text: There’s this sweet spot in software where speed meets quality. You can build fast without cutting corners. The trick? Templated personal software.It’s underrated as hell.Let me explain 🧵 I’ll drop my Makefile template at the end

raw.full_text: There’s this sweet spot in software where speed meets quality. You can build fast without cutting corners. The trick? Templated personal software.It’s underrated as hell.Let me explain 🧵 I’ll drop my Makefile template at the end

text: Versioning bugs later on are frustrating as hell, so I put myself in a position to avoid them 👇- A .python-version file- Use Poetry for package mgmt (points to commits, not just versions)- Have a Makefile with rock-solid logic for install, re-installs, and virtual…

raw.full_text: Versioning bugs later on are frustrating as hell, so I put myself in a position to avoid them 👇- A .python-version file- Use Poetry for package mgmt (points to commits, not just versions)- Have a Makefile with rock-solid logic for install, re-installs, and virtual…

text: I use FastAPI + Pydantic + SQLAlchemy, with a domain-driven structure.Core domains (users, orgs, plugins) are  templated so I get working, secure endpoints as soon as Firebase and Stripe are set up and a valid .env is in place.

raw.full_text: I use FastAPI + Pydantic + SQLAlchemy, with a domain-driven structure.Core domains (users, orgs, plugins) are  templated so I get working, secure endpoints as soon as Firebase and Stripe are set up and a valid .env is in place.

text: In my setup, the API and the database are a single system. Every schema change is code. Every migration is tracked. There’s zero “magic,” and zero chance something drifts out of sync. Here’s how that works 👇- SQLAlchemy + Pydantic form the backbone- Alembic handles schema…

raw.full_text: In my setup, the API and the database are a single system. Every schema change is code. Every migration is tracked. There’s zero “magic,” and zero chance something drifts out of sync. Here’s how that works 👇- SQLAlchemy + Pydantic form the backbone- Alembic handles schema…

text: With Cookiecutter, I can spin up new services in seconds using this template.I  keep all versions in one repo across branches. If I improve the  template and want to apply the changes everywhere I just rebase all my  projects.

raw.full_text: With Cookiecutter, I can spin up new services in seconds using this template.I  keep all versions in one repo across branches. If I improve the  template and want to apply the changes everywhere I just rebase all my  projects.

text: Deploying logic should be templated as well - regardless of how you deploy - using Make commands is a great way of doing this and provides you with flexibility of running it either locally or in CI/CD pipeline in the exact same way.Personally, I deploy to a personal Kubernetes cluster. My flow is simple and fully scripted:- Build Docker artifacts- Re-apply Helm config- Push K8s secrets from .env.prod- Tag the release in GitOne Make command. Same steps every time. No surprises.

raw.full_text: Deploying logic should be templated as well - regardless of how you deploy - using Make commands is a great way of doing this and provides you with flexibility of running it either locally or in CI/CD pipeline in the exact same way.Personally, I deploy to a personal Kubernetes cluster. My flow is simple and fully scripted:- Build Docker artifacts- Re-apply Helm config- Push K8s secrets from .env.prod- Tag the release in GitOne Make command. Same steps every time. No surprises.

text:
I think anything around search would be useful (either key-word based, or LLM based). That was the original rational that motivated the idea:
- I gain interest in a topic
- I'm pretty sure people have already written high quality thread about it! And I'd like to study it
- But just doing tweeter search feels limiting. I would love  to find those high quality niches, and doing so w/ search alone is borderline impossible at times. 
- Would be great if i could somehow filter tweets by value - and bookmarks are a good metric for that

But as the idea marinated other , more interesting use-cases emerged:
- I highly value my bookmarks & their folders, and there are items there that I keep coming back to use as references
- I'm sure other people would find value in some of my folders if they're starting to learn about something that I've been around for a while. 
- Similarly, there are people in this platform that I highly respect and admire from whom I'd love to be able to "steal" some of their bookmarks on given topics. 
- Hell, even just scrolling through their folders and see how they organize their information and the best piece of content that they saw on given topics could bring very interesting material to me.

text: So.... are you familiar with tpot (this part of twitter) - it's a loosely defined, online subculture on Twitter  related with intellectual discourse, tech, altruism movements, ~~vibing ~~. Idk it's hard to describe tbhBut they created this annual meetup @vibecamp_ that from what i heard from testimonies is quite the experience. Always felt compelled to go but never did because 1) US far away and 2) i was mostly just a lurker here and didn't really engage.Jesscamp (created by @frideswyth) is the EU equivalent in sunny Portugal! I feel really alive and energetic lately, and part of that is because of the sense of community I'm getting from here, so I literally cannot wait to see how an in-person version of this feels like.

text:
You have good ideas, but your brain is chaotic by design. Use frameworks to add structure.

E.g: Your Product brings value through Use Cases which are supported by Features. 

Don't mix them up - there's an abstraction layer there that eases your cognitive thinking

raw.full_text:
You have good ideas, but your brain is chaotic by design. Use frameworks to add structure.

E.g: Your Product brings value through Use Cases which are supported by Features. 

Don't mix them up - there's an abstraction layer there that eases your cognitive thinking

text: There’s another useful abstraction between:– What the use-cases actually are– How they’re communicatedDefine use-cases clearly so you don’t lose track of what you're solving (product). How you present them can differ (marketing). Keep both layers in mind.

raw.full_text: There’s another useful abstraction between:– What the use-cases actually are– How they’re communicatedDefine use-cases clearly so you don’t lose track of what you're solving (product). How you present them can differ (marketing). Keep both layers in mind.

text:
We numb ourselves down so much. This is something critical to solve in our own lives, but it's so hard.

Blaming it on tech alone is fair, but also a cop-out. 

Addictive behaviors (like silencing the inner voice) are usually symptoms, not the root cause.

raw.full_text:
We numb ourselves down so much. This is something critical to solve in our own lives, but it's so hard.

Blaming it on tech alone is fair, but also a cop-out. 

Addictive behaviors (like silencing the inner voice) are usually symptoms, not the root cause.

text: What I mean here is: Tech for sure is amplifying the issue, but it's not at its core.So what's at the core? I guess:

raw.full_text: What I mean here is: Tech for sure is amplifying the issue, but it's not at its core.So what's at the core? I guess:

text: It's hard to imagine now, but people had other ways to numb themselves out before smartphones.It's not like everyone was in perfect alignment with themselves before, right? Thing is, the coping tools weren't as easily available & didn’t pull you into such a deep trance.

raw.full_text: It's hard to imagine now, but people had other ways to numb themselves out before smartphones.It's not like everyone was in perfect alignment with themselves before, right? Thing is, the coping tools weren't as easily available & didn’t pull you into such a deep trance.

text: TLDR;1. Inner voice can bring pain or discomfort. 2. We deal with it by shutting it down3. This has short term benefits (no pain)4. If we do this enough times, we teach ourselves this is how we deal with issues.

raw.full_text: TLDR;1. Inner voice can bring pain or discomfort. 2. We deal with it by shutting it down3. This has short term benefits (no pain)4. If we do this enough times, we teach ourselves this is how we deal with issues.

text: Plus, as kids, we probably weren't taught how to sit with difficult feelings - just to distract, suppress, or override them  - and that strategy sticks to adulthood.(to be fair to parents - i think this might something really difficult to teach tbh).

raw.full_text: Plus, as kids, we probably weren't taught how to sit with difficult feelings - just to distract, suppress, or override them  - and that strategy sticks to adulthood.(to be fair to parents - i think this might something really difficult to teach tbh).

text: Our "inner voice" can bring up unresolved thoughts and we’re wired to move away from discomfort, whether physical or emotional (pain used to mean danger). So even emotional discomfort (fear, shame, boredom) triggers avoidance responses.

raw.full_text: Our "inner voice" can bring up unresolved thoughts and we’re wired to move away from discomfort, whether physical or emotional (pain used to mean danger). So even emotional discomfort (fear, shame, boredom) triggers avoidance responses.

text: So the next step is - why’s it so hard to stick with them?They clearly yield positive results when I commit. I love what they bring out in me. But the habit eventually dries up, and coming back feels way harder than just maintaining it.

raw.full_text: So the next step is - why’s it so hard to stick with them?They clearly yield positive results when I commit. I love what they bring out in me. But the habit eventually dries up, and coming back feels way harder than just maintaining it.

text: This probably isn't news to anyone but idk, i'm writing this mostly just to organize my thoughts on it.And I guess the tools for how to fix this aren't new either:- Meditation- Journaling- MindfulnessBut it's so hard to stick with them arrrghhh

text: Ok, this was a decent exercise, i think it's good for now. The lesson is clearly to try and protect these processes at all cost when we feel them fading away. Which doesn't make it any less hard.

raw.full_text: Ok, this was a decent exercise, i think it's good for now. The lesson is clearly to try and protect these processes at all cost when we feel them fading away. Which doesn't make it any less hard.

text: Aaaaand now, i think we close the loop? They're processes that require some level of mental effort (especially when we're in bad mental places) - so if we don't cultivate them it's easy to fall back to [[auto-pilot mode]] and choose to deal with pain by avoiding it.

raw.full_text: Aaaaand now, i think we close the loop? They're processes that require some level of mental effort (especially when we're in bad mental places) - so if we don't cultivate them it's easy to fall back to [[auto-pilot mode]] and choose to deal with pain by avoiding it.

text:
All mental blocks require some form of [[re-framing]] to overcome (looking at something from a different perspective).

This requires a deeper understanding of the issue. 👉That's why introspection is helpful.

But you can't think your way out of everything. You might need to acquire more data through experiments.👉 That's why action is so important.

Also, sometimes, re-framing requires you to prove things to yourself. You can't internalize things by just writing about them. You need to live them.

Also, sometimes, re-framing requires you to prove things to yourself. You can't internalize things by just writing about them. You need to live them.

text:
@goblinodds recently touched on this in a really good way. Her video has lots of interesting bits but regarding that topic:

"""
(...) people worry if they give away the game, the other person is gonna think you're cringe and low status and get the ick. I think this is fake. If there's any chance in hell the other person is gonna like you, they're not gonna be put of but knowing you're into them - if anything they'll become more interested once they realize somebody else is interested.

The thing that's cringe and low status isn't the liking the person, it's needing something from the person. 

People want to be seen, and so if you can do this for people, they're gonna be happy about it. Even if you just see them because you have a crush on them.
"""

https://t.co/HqORsbqAs3

text: So, for a while Europe was under fascism and Portugal was no exception. We were under dictatorship until 1974 - one of the last in Western Europe.

raw.full_text: So, for a while Europe was under fascism and Portugal was no exception. We were under dictatorship until 1974 - one of the last in Western Europe.

text: First thing they took over was the radio station.They played a beautiful song about freedom - Grândola, Vila Morena - which had been banned by the regime.It was used as the signal that the revolution had begun.https://t.co/UOmXDJ8vWC

raw.full_text: First thing they took over was the radio station.They played a beautiful song about freedom - Grândola, Vila Morena - which had been banned by the regime.It was used as the signal that the revolution had begun.https://t.co/UOmXDJ8vWC

text: That year, a group of military officers organized a coup to overthrow the fascist regime. Really hard to pull off -took months to plan, and many were discovered and arrested.But on April 25th, soldiers left their bases and marched toward Lisbon to take down the dictatorship.

raw.full_text: That year, a group of military officers organized a coup to overthrow the fascist regime. Really hard to pull off -took months to plan, and many were discovered and arrested.But on April 25th, soldiers left their bases and marched toward Lisbon to take down the dictatorship.

text: It was one of the most peaceful revolutions in history.The military toppled a 48-year dictatorship in less than a day with almost no shots fired.Soldiers refused to turn on civilians. People flooded the streets in support.

raw.full_text: It was one of the most peaceful revolutions in history.The military toppled a 48-year dictatorship in less than a day with almost no shots fired.Soldiers refused to turn on civilians. People flooded the streets in support.

text: A women that sold flowers on the streets, handed red carnations to troops, who placed them in their rifle barrels. That image became a symbol. https://t.co/dJNfWmOux9

raw.full_text: A women that sold flowers on the streets, handed red carnations to troops, who placed them in their rifle barrels. That image became a symbol. https://t.co/dJNfWmOux9

text:
When I was 13/14 yo I started an online RPG writing game around a teen book collection about spy kids (CHERUB). 

The cringiest things I will ever write are there (I'm pretty sure the first online girlfriend and sexting experience was there).

When I was 13/14 yo I started an online RPG writing game around a teen book collection about spy kids (CHERUB). 

The cringiest things I will ever write are there (I'm pretty sure the first online girlfriend and sexting experience was there).

But it was so fucking cool.

text: And yes it's still online 🫣🫣🫣https://t.co/xsjJY87xCA

raw.full_text: And yes it's still online 🫣🫣🫣https://t.co/xsjJY87xCA

text: How did it work?There was in real time chat, where we just hanged out. Then there were blog threads of comments where we collectively built our adventures. It was essentially co-op writing games.

raw.full_text: How did it work?There was in real time chat, where we just hanged out. Then there were blog threads of comments where we collectively built our adventures. It was essentially co-op writing games.

text: Admins (me on the start but then I let more people in as it grew) would serve as the mission kick-starters (narrators) and than people picked it up from there.Each mission had a limited amount of people and we'd just build out of each other's imagination.

raw.full_text: Admins (me on the start but then I let more people in as it grew) would serve as the mission kick-starters (narrators) and than people picked it up from there.Each mission had a limited amount of people and we'd just build out of each other's imagination.

text:
The game gained an insane amount of organic lore. 

NPCs would make appearances across different missions or events. 

Villains would do surprise mystery come back.

We'd date and crush on each other, because we were a bunch of teens on the internet.

text:
2nd of May - actually I got a way better one yesterday. 

Met a girl at the hostel from Sweeden - she's spending the semester studying in a town close by. 

She also wanted to go on a pub crawl but we both felt it was a bit overpriced. So we schemed to follow the group without having bought bracelet. We made it 2 bars without getting caught.

Than we went to a Salsa club and she taught me the basics of Salsa - which is a bit different from Forró but not thaaaaat much. We danced and talked for a few hours.

Lovely first night, great way to set the pace.

text:
Great experiment.

I (almost) had to raise money a few months ago in a similar fashion and I thought about this for a bit. 

Since we're devs, I assume that using our expertise there is the way to go. 

Since you're on the twitter archive crew, maybe there's something fun that you can do around that? Idk. 

Brain Dumping here assuming that direction:

- Birdseye is so cool and I was always kinda sad that it's only enabled for specific accounts. Maybe you could personally enable it for people that donate!
- Do the same for the banger bot?
- Creating a very ARTSY website based on a person's twitter data could also be quite cool but seems hard.

Thing is maybe these require some computing and you don't realy want to spend money on this since what you want is to raise. So not 100% if this is the way but it's where my head went.

text: I want to unpack this life experiment and share:– How I feel about returning to corporate work– My career plan moving forward– Why the business side didn’t click, and what I’m learning from it

raw.full_text: I want to unpack this life experiment and share:– How I feel about returning to corporate work– My career plan moving forward– Why the business side didn’t click, and what I’m learning from it

text: This isn’t the end of my indie hacking journey.I still want to make progress by:– Staying active here– Meeting people I can learn from or build with– Monitoring tech and business trends– Building tools I believe should exist — and finding honest ways to monetize them

raw.full_text: This isn’t the end of my indie hacking journey.I still want to make progress by:– Staying active here– Meeting people I can learn from or build with– Monitoring tech and business trends– Building tools I believe should exist — and finding honest ways to monetize them

text: What makes the most sense for me career-wise now is to return to product strategy & management. It’s the area I most want to grow in.I already have some experience with it, but there's still a lot to learn.

raw.full_text: What makes the most sense for me career-wise now is to return to product strategy & management. It’s the area I most want to grow in.I already have some experience with it, but there's still a lot to learn.

text: Software development is my bread and butter and I'm really confident in my skills there.But I’m hoping that not doing it in my 9–to-5 will keep my code-fatigue low, so I still have energy to build my own stuff on the side.

raw.full_text: Software development is my bread and butter and I'm really confident in my skills there.But I’m hoping that not doing it in my 9–to-5 will keep my code-fatigue low, so I still have energy to build my own stuff on the side.

text: Why didn’t the business side of my last 6 months work out as well as I hoped?

raw.full_text: Why didn’t the business side of my last 6 months work out as well as I hoped?

text: First, consistency.I chose to prioritize personal growth over full-on execution and I don’t regret that .But building something solo requires aggressive consistency. Living and breathing it. I just wasn’t in that headspace, and that’s okay.

raw.full_text: First, consistency.I chose to prioritize personal growth over full-on execution and I don’t regret that .But building something solo requires aggressive consistency. Living and breathing it. I just wasn’t in that headspace, and that’s okay.

text: Second, I jumped in a bit unprepared.I still lack experience in business strategy, and I made a lot of avoidable mistakes. My ideas weren’t fully formed, and I never committed hard enough to any of them.Excitement isn’t enough - it burns out fast. Real progress takes…

raw.full_text: Second, I jumped in a bit unprepared.I still lack experience in business strategy, and I made a lot of avoidable mistakes. My ideas weren’t fully formed, and I never committed hard enough to any of them.Excitement isn’t enough - it burns out fast. Real progress takes…

text: I also had zero online presence which I feel is a big aspect of this space.

raw.full_text: I also had zero online presence which I feel is a big aspect of this space.

text: Lastly, I’ve realized I do my best work in teams.I feed off collaboration. I get better when I’m surrounded by smart, driven people - and I think I bring energy and clarity to others too. That’s something I’ve missed during this solo phase.

raw.full_text: Lastly, I’ve realized I do my best work in teams.I feed off collaboration. I get better when I’m surrounded by smart, driven people - and I think I bring energy and clarity to others too. That’s something I’ve missed during this solo phase.

text: All in all, I’m not beating myself up. I learned a ton!  And I’m getting clearer on what I need to succeed - both in terms of mindset and environment.  The game isn’t over!

raw.full_text: All in all, I’m not beating myself up. I learned a ton!  And I’m getting clearer on what I need to succeed - both in terms of mindset and environment.  The game isn’t over!

text: After thinking about it for a bit, I guess the biggest challenge for the OMI is to untangle its core findings on idea spreading from the underlying Twitter algorithm🧵

raw.full_text: After thinking about it for a bit, I guess the biggest challenge for the OMI is to untangle its core findings on idea spreading from the underlying Twitter algorithm🧵

text: Idea spreading at a human level is basically a network spreading phenomenon.These days the spread happens through content on the internet. And that content gets pushed by big tech algorithms (like Twitter).

raw.full_text: Idea spreading at a human level is basically a network spreading phenomenon.These days the spread happens through content on the internet. And that content gets pushed by big tech algorithms (like Twitter).

text: My fear is that while digging into human-level idea spreading, we end up mostly reverse-engineering specific big tech algos (Twitter's, for example). Which is still interesting - but then we can end up with tools that don't generalize well for other algos.Or even worse,…

raw.full_text: My fear is that while digging into human-level idea spreading, we end up mostly reverse-engineering specific big tech algos (Twitter's, for example). Which is still interesting - but then we can end up with tools that don't generalize well for other algos.Or even worse,…

text: There are two layers here: - ideas at the human level (beliefs, opinions, stuff that sticks in your head) - content at the internet level (posts, memes, videos). They build off each other, but are not the same thing.

raw.full_text: There are two layers here: - ideas at the human level (beliefs, opinions, stuff that sticks in your head) - content at the internet level (posts, memes, videos). They build off each other, but are not the same thing.

text:
It's a meta-problem, we use the underlying content phenomena to explore the human mind.

Fascinating - but incredibly complex. Kudos to whoever is working on this.

raw.full_text:

X is the world’s biggest open lab for human thought and culture formation.

So, I've crafted a tool that empowers AI to research directly on X and I'm on the hunt for the most exciting use-cases.

Here’s what it can already do and how you can use it 🧵

text:
Twitter data is among the most powerful globally.

With enough volume and modeling, it reveals how ideas spread and culture forms. Also, the world's brightest minds often share insights and thought processes here. It's a gold mine!

However, navigating it manually is tough, as I found in my thesis. But new AI models like GPT-5 and Sonnect 4.5 offer really promising research techniques that might help!

raw.full_text:

text:
Recent updates have enabled third-party connectors to be installed in our own AI platforms (ChatGPT, Claude).

AI models connect to external tools and data sources (databases, internal APIs, SaaS applications) through MCP servers (they're the LLM’s counterpart to traditional APIs providing a secure, standardized, and modular functionality).

I believe we're about to enter a new era of MCP marketplace similar to what the smartphone app store of the mid-2010s was, but that's a story for another post.

raw.full_text:
Recent updates have enabled third-party connectors to be installed in our own AI platforms (ChatGPT, Claude).

AI models connect to external tools and data sources (databases, internal APIs, SaaS applications) through MCP servers (they're the LLM’s counterpart to traditional APIs providing a secure, standardized, and modular functionality).

I believe we're about to enter a new era of MCP marketplace similar to what the smartphone app store of the mid-2010s was, but that's a story for another post.

text:
Long story short: built my own Twitter/X MCP server powered not only by X but also the @comm_archive - an open-source, public infrastructure project created by @exgenesis that aims to collect, host, and make accessible digital social data.

The digital archive is required as a way to access a higher volume of data, due to harsh restraints from the official API. A lot of the most incredible people on the platform have already uploaded their datasets!

raw.full_text:
Long story short: built my own Twitter/X MCP server powered not only by X but also the @comm_archive - an open-source, public infrastructure project created by @exgenesis that aims to collect, host, and make accessible digital social data.

The digital archive is required as a way to access a higher volume of data, due to harsh restraints from the official API. A lot of the most incredible people on the platform have already uploaded their datasets!

text:
Conclusion:

Research capabilities show potential but it's still not outstanding. It requires good seeds to work properly (topic + twitter handles to target).

Requiring a seed is fine and honestly expected in such a massive corpus, but it should be able to go further away from it than it is right now.

I need to maximize the amount of value gathered by a single request. When the model shows interest in a specific tweet, i should grab not only the full thread of the OP but also the top-k replies.

I also need to streamline the amount of filters available for search. The models make too many sub-optimal requests with poor filter clauses which ends up requiring trial-and-error, errors, noise, etc. The complexity involved in getting to good data is hurting their research process.

If there's any other piece of feedback you think I should consider feel free to mention it!

If you've read this far, you're likely curious about the tool.

To give it a try, I'm offering free access to a read-only version. This allows you to explore interesting use-cases and help shape the tool by providing feedback. Here's how you can gain access:

- Send me a direct message, and I'll evaluate access requests on a case-by-case basis.
- Contribute €5 or more to its maintenance and development via https://t.co/w30L3ROb7l.

If you make a financial contribution and wish to explore more uses beyond just research, I can set up a private instance just for you. This way, you can schedule tweets and interact with your personal data (if you provide  the necessary access).

text:
I guess it varies by person or project whether you're more motivated by “Purpose” or “Crew” (with the obvious caveat that both are important, and you can always aim for a combination of both).

I always thought of myself as more purpose-driven (feeling like I was missing the right project to bring meaning to it all), but reflecting on my past experiences, the times I pushed hardest and felt great were always because I was motivated by those around me.

Also, “crew” seems more objective, palpable, and dynamic than “purpose” - which requires such a deep level of self-awareness and introspection .

text:
Incredibly on point!

I don’t consider myself particularly gifted, but I can identify this phenomenon in people around me.

Ironically, I think being smart but not particularly gifted is a greater advantage in life given the current landscape.

In late college, I realized the huge competitive advantage from:

- relying on groups to grow
- relying on mentors for decision-making
- putting your ego aside and being okay with asking for help loudly.

Suddenly, people I admired immensely were impressed with what I achieved by leveraging these and asked how I had done it.

- You need to be smart enough to feel that winning doesn’t seem impossible - it seems achievable, just really hard.
- But not so smart that you take winning mostly for granted.

And that resourcefulness ends up generalizing much better then raw intelligence

text:
I'm generally a happy and fulfilled person.

I usually get what I want out of life because I'm driven, mostly competent, creative, communicative, fortunate, and very lucky.

And yet there's usually a sense that *something's missing*. It's not ALWAYS there, but it's never too far off either.

At times, I thought it was because I needed a  partner, or because I wasn't working on something ambitious or meaningful enough. My family was suffocating me. I didn't have the right people around me. I was too fat. I wasn't confident enough. Etc, etc.

I think this is a very common problem out there.

This year, I gave myself the freedom to do and chase whatever I felt like - and still ended up  with ~~that feeling~~.

I'm sensing that a big part of it is lack of focus.  Not necessarily focus *on* something, but a general lack of awareness. I catch myself acting on instinct without a clear sense of why I'm doing what I'm doing.

If I have 100 paths ahead of me, I:
> feel overwhelmed by having to choose one
> probably pick the first promising one without giving it much thought to overcome the anxiety
>regret it
> switch to another
> repeat this a few times, ultimately not committing properly to any.

As I re-read this, the solution seems obvious: build a solid routine with plenty of meditation, exercise and journaling - practises I already know to be great grounding mechanisms. 

And yet, as I re-read it again, I notice this links to stuff I've read recently and that I might be missing an important piece: a supportive context. Maybe I need to tune my crew to help me overcome my struggles around focus.

And as I re-re-read it, I notice the irony that I opened the post complaining about constantly lacking *something* and my instinct to *identify and fill it*, then I spent the rest doing *exactly* that. But you know, maybe that's just life. Maybe you're meant to always look for *more*. 

Wait, am I just lacking a gratitude journal?!?!

Ok, I'll stop... 

---

Anyway, as my sabbatical is coming to an end, I'd say it was a success in certain aspects and a failure in others. 

It definitely changed my life's trajectory and I'll treasure this period deeply. I recognise how incredibly lucky I am to have been in a position to do it.

Life is changing once again in the next few weeks, and I'll do my best to seize it.

And yet there's usually a sense that *something's missing*. It's not ALWAYS there, but it's never too far off either.

At times, I thought it was because I needed a  partner, or because I wasn't working on something ambitious or meaningful enough. My family was suffocating me. I didn't have the right people around me. I was too fat. I wasn't confident enough. Etc, etc.

I think this is a very common problem out there.

This year, I gave myself the freedom to do and chase whatever I felt like - and still ended up  with ~~that feeling~~.

I'm sensing that a big part of it is lack of focus.  Not necessarily focus *on* something, but a general lack of awareness. I catch myself acting on instinct without a clear sense of why I'm doing what I'm doing.

If I have 100 paths ahead of me, I:
> feel overwhelmed by having to choose one
> probably pick the first promising one without giving it much thought to overcome the anxiety
>regret it
> switch to another
> repeat this a few times, ultimately not committing properly to any.

As I re-read this, the solution seems obvious: build a solid routine with plenty of meditation, exercise and journaling - practises I already know to be great grounding mechanisms. 

And yet, as I re-read it again, I notice this links to stuff I've read recently and that I might be missing an important piece: a supportive context. Maybe I need to tune my crew to help me overcome my struggles around focus.

And as I re-re-read it, I notice the irony that I opened the post complaining about constantly lacking *something* and my instinct to *identify and fill it*, then I spent the rest doing *exactly* that. But you know, maybe that's just life. Maybe you're meant to always look for *more*. 

Wait, am I just lacking a gratitude journal?!?!

text:
Brilliant stuff right here.

This kinda suggests that "finding meaning" is trivial, but achieving anything in quadrant four is way harder than it looks since it requires effective coordination.

- Everything at the `Alone` lane "only" requires you to master your own will.
- Things in the `Consuming Together` quadrant are easy since they're (mostly) low effort. Just ask enough people enough times, and you'll get there.

But `Creating Together` demands a more long-term commitment that tests people's will. It requires effective coordination and communication, sometimes leadership.

It requires the "right" people with the "right" synergy.

I think I'm above average at kickstarting initiatives, but not great at maintaining them. It's something that I've been realising this year, that I want to get better on.

Should probably do some post-mortems.

text:
Been noticing, so much toxicity in the family lately.

For years, some relationships have been really superficial. People tolerate and are cordial with each other face-to-face, but then shit talk in private.

And since I’m the young, chill, easy-to-talk-to person they all vent out to me.

When I was younger, I felt a huge responsibility for having this role. I tried to fix things under the hood as best I could, shape mindsets, share perspectives, and guide people toward a more harmonious place.

But things never got better, and as they got older they started losing their filter and really criticize one another so rudely, so bluntly, so hatred filled, they've been complaining about the same things for decades now and are incapable of communicating like adults.

I’m so tired of being their dumping ground for toxicity. It feels like all my attempts to improve things are meaningless because they’re too proud and too set in their ways to be influenced by someone they see as just a kid.

Talking to them leaves me so frustrated, outraged and cynical for days….

text:
It's 2025 WRAPPING season.

This year, I actively and consistently tweeted after four years of lurking, and I feel like I've only just scratched the surface of what online communities have to offer!

I will keep sharing my internal state and let the universe guide me through your replies!

> The only way forward is inwards, and the only inwards is through expression!

Special thanks to my ride or dies!

To @woocassh and @fullstackmiguel for being some of the most genuine folks in the very superficial space of indie hacking!

To @IaimforGOAT for a great IRL friendship and a wonderfull adventurous week in Porto!

To @lapislagoons and @rachelclif for filling my TL with wonderful, genuine, heart felt wisdom and art.

And to anyone else who's participating in this crazy social game around here!

See you in the TL in 2026!

Im working on something that enables this and it’s already quite functional. 

Main issue is that to get this to work on twitter data you need to ingest lots of historical data and that can be kind of expensive (0.005€ per tweet).

Community archive fixes the money problem entirely but brings in the issue that it o my works for a small subset of accounts.

But yeah, just the semantic search alone is awesome - works way better then traditional twitter search. 

My vision is to support Substack as well.

If you’re interested in it lmk - i can add you to a betta version!

text:
I understand what you’re saying, but i don’t think it needs to be as strongly enforced and specific as you’re envisioning it.

Technically speaking - you can only pivot if you had some sort of plan to begin with.

I think trying to outline something, pushes you to identify what you want, if you’re being over-ambitious with your time, not ambitious enough.

Like one of the best things of 2025 was indeed Jesscamp, and I was wondering how i could put myself in a situation to have more of that in 2026 so I ended up scrolling through casa tilo’s events and found super interesting stuff. 

I was also trying to see what country I should aim to visit and found out that next year is one of the best to go see the Northen Lights because of the solar cycles.

I don’t think any of this has necessarily to do with yearly planning - just the usual, bring attention into life, your wants, your needs - but this is generally a great reflective time so… yeah?

text:
I resonate with this line of thinking.

But designing processes for yourself is particularly challenging because you need to serve as the designer, actor, manager, and reviewer. It's difficult for your mind to juggle all these roles simultaneously + it's hard to see the full picture when you're in the midst of it.

I'm somewhat of a one-trick pony when it comes to creating processes:

• I make it a daily activity for a set period of time (e.g. 45m workout for 75 days - any physical activity counts).
• It needs to be daily because if the frequency is lower (e.g., weekly), I don't generate enough momentum to feel the urgency, and eventually, life gets in the way and there goes the process.
• Whether I can make it stick or not is very much decided in the first 2-3 weeks, so this is the period you need to be more mindful of.
• From experience, whether this approach works or not can still feel like a coin toss.
• At best, you'll follow the process perfectly for its duration and then feel its effects for a while afterward. If you're smart, you'll set and respect a new period afterward to maintain the process.

I don't think this is perfect at all - which is why I'd love other perspectives around this - I think I got this framework from all my past attempts at 75 Hard which yielded great results so it stuck with me, but it's somehwat fragile.

raw.full_text:
I resonate with this line of thinking.

But designing processes for yourself is particularly challenging because you need to serve as the designer, actor, manager, and reviewer. It's difficult for your mind to juggle all these roles simultaneously + it's hard to see the full picture when you're in the midst of it.

I'm somewhat of a one-trick pony when it comes to creating processes:

• I make it a daily activity for a set period of time (e.g. 45m workout for 75 days - any physical activity counts).
• It needs to be daily because if the frequency is lower (e.g., weekly), I don't generate enough momentum to feel the urgency, and eventually, life gets in the way and there goes the process.
• Whether I can make it stick or not is very much decided in the first 2-3 weeks, so this is the period you need to be more mindful of.
• From experience, whether this approach works or not can still feel like a coin toss.
• At best, you'll follow the process perfectly for its duration and then feel its effects for a while afterward. If you're smart, you'll set and respect a new period afterward to maintain the process.

I don't think this is perfect at all - which is why I'd love other perspectives around this - I think I got this framework from all my past attempts at 75 Hard which yielded great results so it stuck with me, but it's somehwat fragile.

I found myself giving the same piece of advice a few times 👇

raw.full_text:
I inspired some friends to journal at the psychedelic festival we attend every 2 years.

And I noticed they easily became obsessed (and eventually bored) with documenting each event in its EXACT chronological order.

I found myself giving the same piece of advice a few times 👇

text:
Journalling 101:

> Don't focus as much on what happened and in which order - focus on how you felt and why.

> Instead of chronologically, try to describe the most intensive stuff first - what experience fetl the most meaningful? Why?

To do that in a way that feels natural, you are forced to describe just enough of the external reality to give structure to the feeling.

So you end up describing the minimum amount of reality needed to anchor the internal truth to something "real".

text:
A few things that feel true, but I'm having a hard time embedding into the thread:

• Stories and narratives are infrastructure for turning emotions and ideals into shared action.

• Stories are infrastructure for collective coordination.  We collectively use them to align around values without centralised control.

• Storytelling is about adding structure so meaning can survive transmission.

@isabelunraveled writes this brilliantly - story telling is modern witchcraft!

text:
Here's mine:

https://t.co/3ElWLlLk55

Engaged across a decade-long arc from nuanced socio-political commentary to technically deep, hands-on software engineering and indie entrepreneurship. Early interests included critical geopolitics—especially China-Hong Kong dynamics—and music culture (notably Roger Waters/Pink Floyd), blending political skepticism with progressive values and a global, multicultural outlook shaped by extensive travel (Beijing, European circuits) and multilingual fluency (Portuguese, English, Mandarin).

 Academic trajectory centered on computer science with a master’s focus on machine learning, deep learning, and data science research. Emphasized rigorous coding standards, reproducible research, and knowledge management using cutting-edge productivity tools (Roam Research, Notion), extending into experimentation with AI models including GPT-3. Developed interdisciplinary analogies linking AI to human cognition and software testing metaphors, reflecting intellectual humility and a penchant for systematizing complex epistemologies. 

Professionally evolved into software engineering with specialization in blockchain, decentralized finance, and Site Reliability Engineering (SRE). Demonstrated expertise in building scalable, instrumented infrastructure incorporating observability-driven development, SLO/SLO policies, and operational excellence. Advocated pragmatic, modular, clean codebases with strong cross-functional collaboration informed by Conway’s law, balancing engineering best practices against organizational culture and product goals. 

Active involvement in financial and crypto markets showed nuanced skepticism: favoring long-term ETFs alongside opportunistic crypto participation while critiquing speculative hype and extrapolating systemic risk from Bitcoin mass adoption. Followed DeFi platforms and DAO governance as potential models for transparency and anti-corruption, yet balanced optimism with pragmatic awareness of scalability and economic realities. 

Technical curiosity expanded toward instrumentation, monitoring, and cutting-edge visualization tools integrating database time series and cloud infrastructure, frequently drawing on distributed systems and containerization (Docker, Kubernetes). Showcased thoughtful engagement with developer experience (DevEx), focusing on automation, templated reusable APIs, and modern tooling ecosystems (Firebase Auth, Swagger, Helm, Stripe). Demonstrated continual adaptation from academic research to business and product contexts with a strong solo indie developer and freelancer profile after leaving corporate employment. 

Cognitive and philosophical interests enriched by active exploration of knowledge graphs, note-taking evolution, and mindful workflows. Strong affinity for system thinking spanning graph theory, neuroscience, and leadership psychology—including Jungian and António Damásio influences—while embracing mindfulness, emotional openness, and flow states. Maintained consistent journaling and handwritten reflection practices to anchor mental clarity and intrinsic motivation over performative digital presence. 

Culturally and socially diverse: cosmopolitan ties to Lisbon, Berlin, and wider European/Portuguese networks merged with global nomadism extending through South America and Asia. Prioritized authentic, experiential richness through travel, music preferences (Porter Robinson, Caroline Polachek), martial arts (jiu-jitsu), and gaming (Dark Souls, Elden Ring), alongside psychedelic culture and trance states explored as apex human experiences. Socially warm and playful online persona balancing frank humor, vulnerability, and positive energy. 

Philosophically engaged with storytelling as foundational to meaning—balancing science and religion as complementary explanatory frameworks—and committed to ethically navigating information ecosystems. Practiced conscious digital attention management and skepticism of algorithmic opacity; advocated for community-validated truth and long-form, high-signal knowledge sharing over social media superficiality. 

Entrepreneurially active in tech product management and early-stage startups, leveraging AI tools like ChatGPT for productivity and workflow automation while emphasizing balance between control, agency, and communal resonance in vocation. Embraced flexible, passion-driven organizational models such as co-ops and social ventures, refraining from traditional VC hype in favor of authentic, incremental growth and accountability. 

Personality marked by introspection, philosophical depth, and self-awareness interlaced with warmth and humor. Navigated challenges of solo indie work: scope management, marketing savvy, content consistency, and personal branding. Open about mental health, work-life balance, and existential inquiry, viewing grit as essential but joy and authenticity as non-negotiable. 

Overall, a globally-informed, multilingual, deeply analytical technologist and entrepreneur with dual fluency in software craftsmanship and systemic thinking. Maintains a cosmopolitan yet grounded lifestyle, integrating decades of socio-political awareness, academic rigor, blockchain innovation, and human-centered mindfulness. Continually explores the interplay of technology, personal development, and community building while prioritizing mental clarity, intrinsic motivation, and authentic social connection.

Philosophically engaged with storytelling as foundational to meaning—balancing science and religion as complementary explanatory frameworks—and committed to ethically navigating information ecosystems. Practiced conscious digital attention management and skepticism of algorithmic opacity; advocated for community-validated truth and long-form, high-signal knowledge sharing over social media superficiality. 

Entrepreneurially active in tech product management and early-stage startups, leveraging AI tools like ChatGPT for productivity and workflow automation while emphasizing balance between control, agency, and communal resonance in vocation. Embraced flexible, passion-driven organizational models such as co-ops and social ventures, refraining from traditional VC hype in favor of authentic, incremental growth and accountability. 

Personality marked by introspection, philosophical depth, and self-awareness interlaced with warmth and humor. Navigated challenges of solo indie work: scope management, marketing savvy, content consistency, and personal branding. Open about mental health, work-life balance, and existential inquiry, viewing grit as essential but joy and authenticity as non-negotiable. 

Overall, a globally-informed, multilingual, deeply analytical technologist and entrepreneur with dual fluency in software craftsmanship and systemic thinking. Maintains a cosmopolitan yet grounded lifestyle, integrating decades of socio-political awareness, academic rigor, blockchain innovation, and human-centered mindfulness. Continually explores the interplay of technology, personal development, and community building while prioritizing mental clarity, intrinsic motivation, and authentic social connection.

text:
[AI Agentic Enablement]

Imo this is where the true potential of the product lives.

Let's use AI to improve search and research around the sources of information we hold so dearly!

Imagine getting coached on specific topics based on the thousands of micro-blogs your favorite thinkers have put out over the years.

You bookmark someone's thread on burnout, productivity spirals, or creative blocks... what if you could access their ENTIRE perspective on that topic? Not just what you happened to see, but the full evolution of their thinking.

Claude Code is incredible at search and problem solving with local files.

Can it be equally powerful as a writing assistant if it knows the topics you value, your past takes on given topics, and the sources you trust the most about them?

That's the direction I'm most excited about. Using AI not to replace your thinking, but to surface the right wisdom from your personal knowledge graph at exactly the right moment.

text:
Helm = package manager for Kubernetes. 

One command (helm install clawdbot) deploys your entire stack with all dependencies, config, secrets, persistent volumes. Plus it automatically handles scaling, availability, health, port forwarding, standard security.

Users get: one-click install, easy upgrades, instant rollbacks, GitOps-ready (ArgoCD/Flux). Works on ANY k8s: GKE, EKS, home labs, k3s.

text:
It’s raining in Lisbon
(a lot)
But somehow getting caught by it is the best part of my day

I deliberately take my headphones off as I leave the gym. I don’t want the music to rob me of this

It’s one of those days that I envy the narrator from fight club
I envy the realness of a punch, of a fight
Yesterday’s valued peace is today’s numbness
But I don’t know what changed

The thin layer of water on the ground is impossible to miss and my feet get wet under my shoes.
The lights of the city get reflected in the puddles.
The car horns contemplate the sound of rain drops.
Everything is alive around me.

I try to play a game in my head where I identify what’s in my peripheral awareness, but it’s too hard. 
There’s too much trying to grab my attention.

I stop walking and close my eyes.
I lift my chin up and for a few seconds try to feel every single rain drop that’s hitting my face.
That feels good
It feels grounding.
But there’s also clear some level of physical discomfort, so I don’t stay there very long.

As I get close to my house, I feel an urge to write and the text starts forming in my hard

> It’s raining in Lisbon, and somehow getting caught by it is the best part of my day
> Yesterday’s peace became today’s numbness and I don’t know what changed
>I feel apathetic and I don’t know why
> There’s a lense like nature to attention where I can focus in and out

I take my coat off and it’s dripping
I get naked as I’m getting ready to shower
But somehow, writing feels more urgent.

I've been meaning to write for so long now.
I sit at the desk
My spirit hungers to create something that’s not code
Something that doesn't happen inside a screen
(eventhough i’m still writing this in a screen, it doesn’t feel like it)

It’s coming from a different place
It feels like i’m hand-writing it.
My spirit feels satisfied
I’m so glad I wrote

Somehow it wasn’t that bad of a day
But it really felt like it was at a point

How curious

text:
Since I became active, I've only posted things that feel like they need to get out.

Not content strategy. Just thoughts that feel true enough they demand to be said. 

This has made it relatively easy for me to hit post. But also lead to content that's really self-centric and.... not very interesting? I feel like.

Makes me feel like I should put more effort into some of content? Have stronger stances on stuff. Contemplate how it will make people feel? 

Idk.... writing this makes me think that maybe I'm not a very interesting person :(

Buuuuut my empirical experience of being me tells me that I am, and I might not be capturing it properly. And I'm not sure how to focus to improve that. Always be posting at random isn't cutting it.

Things get significantly easier for me when I frame them as:

> I need to do X because someone I care about needs me to do it.

When it’s just for me, there’s more friction. More negotiation. A higher chance I just… don’t do it. But when it’s for someone else, that friction disappears.

Looking back, this pattern shows up in multiple moments of my life. The clearest one is from 2023, when I lost 10kg.

During workouts, I would imagine two of my closest friends hyping me. Like… literally. I imagined them in a commentator booth talking to me through my headphones commenting my workouts, pushing me. It was entirely in my head, but it felt real enough to change how I showed up.

Writing it out now, it sounds slightly crazy. I don’t think I’ve ever told anyone about this except for them.

But the important part is this: I wasn’t framing that journey purely as something I was doing for myself. Somewhere along the way, it became “I’m doing this for them.”. Which is irrational, because the outcome was mine. But the mechanism worked.

I think I’ve known this about myself for a while. I just never articulated it this clearly. And now that I have, it feels… useful, like a lever I can pull. But also slightly dangerous.

Because if this is true, it raises an uncomfortable question: do I undervalue my own needs? Do I require external anchors to fully commit to things that should already matter to me?

This became more visible recently, partly through things my girlfriend pointed out, and partly through therapy. I’ve known that other people’s perceptions influence how I see myself. What I didn’t realize is how much they can influence my motivation.

Not just how I feel — but what I actually do.

So now I’m left with a more practical question:

If I know this about myself, can I use it deliberately?

Can I “trick” my brain into adopting this frame for things I want to do — like writing daily, or meditating — by tying them to other people? There’s a common idea around this: do it for your future family, your partner, the people who love you. Become better for them.

Intellectually, it makes sense. I agree with it. But I’m not sure it’s concrete enough to activate this mechanism. The workouts worked because the people felt present. So the real question becomes:

Can this be engineered?

Can I create frames that are specific and vivid enough to consistently reduce that friction — without losing the fact that, ultimately, I’m doing these things for myself?

Or is there a risk that, by leaning too much on others, I drift further away from building intrinsic motivation?

I don’t have an answer yet.

But it feels like something worth understanding properly.

text:
There’s a whole AI system architecture waiting to be written down. 

The analogies to standard software are starting to manifest, and naming them properly helps while designing.

The stack as I see it:

• LLMs as the CPU. The processor executing instructions.

• Harnesses as the OS. Claude Code, Cursor, and friends are operating systems wrapped around the LLM-CPU. They provide the syscalls (tool use), the file system (workspace), memory management (context window), and process control (sub-agents).

• Skills as functions. Small, composable, callable units of behavior the model invokes on demand.

• Plugins as packages. Bundles of skills shipped together.

• AGENTS.md and context files as config (.bashrc, package.json, env vars). Declarative state that shapes the runtime before any “code” runs.

The interfaces between these layers aren’t settled yet. 

• What does a skill’s “type signature” look like? 
• How do plugins declare dependencies? • What’s the ABI between harness and LLM? 

It’s the wild west right now — which is incredibly fun — but the patterns are starting to crystallize.

text:
A real ABI is exact if you violate it you get a segfault. 

An LLM “ABI” is fuzzy since models tolerate malformed tool calls, recover from weird formatting, etc.

The open question: does this stay fuzzy, or does it harden? MCP is a bet that it should harden (real protocol with a spec). The alternative future is that models get good enough at inferring conventions that the ABI stays loose forever and we just… vibe-compile?!

Anthropic, OpenAI, and open-source models all went in a different direction so far and an harness built for one format breaks on another, but I would imagine that this should kinda convert eventually?

text:
Frank Herbert basically called this back in 1965. The whole premise of Dune starts after a massive "Jihad" against thinking machines, and the main conclusion was that humans get lazy when machines do the heavy lifting. 

> "Men turned their minds over to machines in the hope that this would set them free. But that only allowed other men with machines to enslave them."

Once they ditched the computers, humanity went inward. They started focusing on "human qualities" to a point that looked like magic.

You had the Bene Gesserit "witches" who tuned their senses so finely they could detect a heartbeat's change from across a room, and Mentats who turned their own brains into biological supercomputers. They used the Spice - a massive psychedelic catalyst - to unlock "ancestral memory," essentially tapping into a deep, "in-the-body" knowledge to navigate the future.

The process of struggling with a thought is exactly what builds the person. The maximum potential of your humanity is something you earn.

The Emperor’s elite soldiers trained on a planet so harsh it killed most of them; the Fremen were even tougher because Arrakis was worse. The "cushion" of the machine is what makes people soft, while the friction of reality makes them supernatural.

If you let an LLM do your thinking, you’re essentially surrendering that evolution.

text:
What I’m thinking for this is:

> You develop a sort of “Intelectual blueprint” context file. Topics that move you, frameworks for thinking, frameworks for replying - the manifesto for your online persona. 

> Pipe that into an Agents.md

> You do an introspective rants every day 
- voice based! Just record what you’re feeling, what you’re struggling, strong emotions. A diary entry that’s voice recorded for convenience.

> An agent that stores these entries as text with a strong memory plugin

> Extend the agent with the ability pull live data from twitter and Substack and cross relate the topics that are on top of your mind with what your mutuals are talking about. And suggests things that you should interact with.

> Some sort of visual UI for you to go over these and be able to consume, dump your thoughts (using recordings as well), quickly get from thoughts to viable replies or original piece of content based on what you’re consuming.

Replies and conversation occur naturally from there.

You get: 
• Intentional content consumption
• Low friction, community powered introspection
• Continuous authentic Content

text:
You can just ask for a hug from your fellow men.

You can just tell someone they’re stuck in a loop, chasing a rite of passage that never seems to come.

You can just let someone know that their book touched you deeply — thanking them for it and fangirling a little.

You can just scheme on the dance floor, and plot to break the circle of social awkwardness and loosen everyone up.

You can just cry and let yourself be held by strangers and heal from a broken heart and a marriage that never came to be.

You can just plot a massive rocketship launch on a little farm in rural Portugal, named after the mythologised birthplace of tantric Buddhism.

You can just sneak into the Makban and join a group cuddle session with strangers — and find yourself pondering whether your girlfriend would approve (I swear it was purely pets!).

You can just follow a procession with no idea where it’s going, end up in a haunted mill, and befriend the spirits that inhabit it by letting your inner caveman loose — dancing and singing like a savage.

You can just let go of your family trauma, and confess that you feel depleted by the constant effort of trying to fix your broken family.

You can just embody the call to adventure for everyone around you. OR tou can decide you’ll accept ALL the adventure calls the camp throws your way.

You can try to be King. Or you can confess to the sage with the beautiful, intense dark eyes that you actually DON’T want to be king — that you fear the responsibility.

You can befriend the other person trying to escape the intensity of the group gatherings, ask them to sit down, share a coffee, and learn all about their nomadic travels around the world.

You can send someone a message telling them you’re missing them at camp.

You can miss your girlfriend, call her on the comedown, and one-shot a beautiful poem for her.

You can venture out to the river during the dark, swim naked after the sauna, and hug your friend afterwards — thanking them for being your call to adventure.

You can seek refuge of the intensity of the camp in the kitchen vibes and helping to cook dinner.

You can ask someone what’s the most intense story they have from their clubbing weekends in Berlin.

You can catch the bus even when you arrive 5m late! And even so, you can still miss the bus by getting distracted during a stop and finding that the bus went ahead without you. You can ask strangers for a ride back to Lisbon and laugh at how hilarious the whole thing is.

And you can tweet from a stranger’s car, reminiscing on what a great time you had this weekend.

I’ll see you on the TL fam 🫶

text:
Elite schools are now introducing AI in quite interesting ways.

It’s quite obvious that this will eventually become the norm, but when it does these interesting approaches will need to be adapted for the masses and stripped of all the magic that optimized for creativity and agency.

After that, we’ll soon get elite schools that will for the most part forbid the use of AI. I think they’ll resemble the traditional education methods we grew up with and optimize for critical thinking.

The difference in ability between kids that come out of both schools will be astronomical as one of the sides will struggle hard with basic decision making - we can see this nowadays, with the “how should I reply…” prompts. We’re slowly outsourcing our critical thinking process .

I can’t even imagine how hard it will be to compete in such an environment. We’ll be at a place where most decisions default to the mean  and there will be massive value for anyone that’s able to provide outlier solutions. Which is, and always be, the case - it’ll just be massively amplified.

text:
I found the decision to put the Buddha above Jesus in this chart a bit edgy but it felt right to me. Why?

· The buddha claimed NO divine status. His enlightenment was a DIY project of brutal introspection. He mapped human consciousness entirely from the inside out. Jesus’s wisdom is bound to divine authority and faith.

· The buddha acts like a cognitive scientist, giving a literal medical diagnosis for how the mind traps itself (and the steps to fix it). Jesus is a relational revolutionary, teaching a radical, poetic ethic of how we treat each other through love and empathy.

So buddha gets the edge here because his path was entirely self-authored: One is a clinical manual for the mind and the other is a radical mandate for the heart
